steps.title: The Windmill Pattern
steps.text:
The **windmill** is a relentless cycle of *discovered checks* that lets you snatch piece after piece while the enemy king has no time to breathe. It is one of the most beautiful — and most lethal — patterns in chess.

## The visual trigger

A windmill needs a very specific setup:

- A **rook or queen** giving check (or about to give check) on the seventh or eighth rank.
- A **bishop** (or sometimes a knight) sitting on a square where it both *delivers a discovered check* and *retreats safely*.
- An enemy king with **only one or two flight squares**, so it must keep returning to the same place.
- **Loose enemy pieces** along the rank or diagonal that the rook scoops up between checks.

## The geometry

The rook and bishop are the two grinding stones of the mill:

1. The bishop steps off the line — **discovered check** from the rook.
2. The king has no choice but to move to a single safe square.
3. The bishop **steps back**, capturing a piece on the way.
4. The rook *checks again* (or the bishop reveals a new check).
5. Repeat until the enemy army is gone.

## The most famous example

**Torre vs. Lasker, Moscow 1925** — the bishop on f6 and rook on g7 ground Lasker's pieces to dust over a dozen moves. Look it up after this lesson; it is unforgettable.

> A windmill turns the laws of chess into a meat grinder.

steps.title: How to Set One Up
steps.text:
Windmills do not happen by accident. They are *engineered*. Once you know the recipe, you start to see them long before they appear.

## The ingredients

- A **bishop** placed on a long diagonal aimed at the enemy king's pocket.
- Your **rook (or queen)** infiltrating to the seventh rank or back rank.
- The enemy king cornered with limited escape squares.
- Enemy pieces lined up along the rook's rank — easy targets between checks.

## The procedure

1. Place the **bishop** behind your rook so that moving the rook delivers a discovered check.
2. Drive the king to a square with no flight option except back where it started.
3. Move the rook *off* the discovered-check square (capturing material), check resolves.
4. Move the rook *back* with check again.
5. Continue until the position is winning.

## How to spot windmill *threats*

- Two of your long-range pieces share an attack on the king.
- The king has only one safe square between the two attackers.
- One of your pieces can capture and return without being captured.

> If you ever wondered why bishops on the long diagonal are scary, the windmill is the answer.
